coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor and smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform based on oxidation induced Ce(III)/Ce(IV) phosphatase-like activity and complexation effect activation for sarcosine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Linjie
Zheng, Shujun
Cai, Zhihao
Wang, Fei
Li, Caolong
coredata.dc:description: Prostate cancer (PCa) has seriously affected men's quality of life and even endangered their lives. Thus, precise and reliable determination of related biomarkers in clinical samples is extremely important for early diagnosis and treatment of PCa. Sarcosine (Sar) is widely regarded as a potential biomarker for non-invasive diagnosis of PCa owing to its specific high-level expression in the development and metastasis of PCa. With the aid of Ce(III) as a bridge between 4-Methylumbelliferyl Phosphate (4-MUP) and orange emitted carbon dots (o-CDs), here we have constructed a new ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Sar determination by reasonably combining the phosphatase-like activity and complexation reaction characteristics of Ce(IV). This sensor indicates high sensitivity, exceptional selectivity, good reliability, and wide linear range of 0.2–120 μM with a detection limit of 89 nM. Besides, our proposed sensor demonstrates excellent precise and stability even in complex actual urine samples. Interestingly, an inexpensive, portable but reliable smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform has been fabricated and successfully employed for precise determination of Sar in urine samples, which offers a powerful and convenient tool for the early diagnosis of PCa, and even inspires the research and development of other practical optical sensing devices for cancer home screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prostate cancer
Sarcosine
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of orange emitted carbon dots (o-CDs)
Fluorescence detection of H2O2 and Sar
Determination of Sar via smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform
Detection of Sar in real samples
Characterization of o-CDs after interacting with Ce(IV)
Sensing mechanism and detection of H2O2
Smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform for Sar determination

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region ( XZ202301YD0018C ). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, fluorescence lifetime tests, and so on.

The authors thank the financial support from the Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region (XZ202301YD0018C). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, fluorescence lifetime tests, and so on.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis route (A) and TEM image (Inset: particle size distribution) (B) of o-CDs. The FT-IR spectra (C), high-resolution C 1 s (D), N 1 s (E), and O 1 s (F) XPS spectra of o-CDs. (G) The UV−vis and fluorescence spectra of o-CDs (Inset: the images under natural light and 365 nm UV light). (H) The fluorescence behavior of o-CDs under different excitation signals.

(A) The TEM image of o-CDs after treatment with Ce(IV). (B) The UV–vis profiles of various systems. The FT-IR spectrum (C), high-resolution Ce 3d (D), C 1 s (E), O 1 s (F), and N 1 s (G) XPS spectra of o-CDs after interacting with Ce(IV). (H) Illustration of H2O2 detection via o-CDs + Ce(III) system.

. (A) The fluorescence spectra of 4-MUP + o-CDs and 4-MUP + o-CDs + Ce(III) + H2O2 systems. (B) Sensing mechanism for H2O2 determination. (C) Fluorescence spectra of 4-MUP + o-CDs + Ce(III) system with different levels of H2O2. (D) Dependence of F578/F448 on content of H2O2. (E) Physical photo for H2O2 with various concentrations under 365 nm UV lamp. (F) Linear relationship between 1/(F578/F448) and the content of H2O2.

. (A) Schematic diagram of Sar sensing through proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor. (B) The fluorescence profiles of 4-MUP + o-CDs and 4-MUP + o-CDs + Ce(III) + Sar + SOX systems. (C) The fluorescence spectra of 4-MUP + o-CDs + Ce(III) + SOX system with various contents of Sar. (D) Dependence of F578/F448 on Sar level in the scope of 0.2–150 μM. (E) Physical photo for various levels of Sar under 365 nm UV lamp. (F) Linear relationship between 1/(F578/F448) and Sar content. (G) Ratiometric fluorometric response of Sar and other species toward 4-MUP + o-CDs + Ce(III) + SOX system.
. (A) The structure diagram of smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform. (B) The operation process diagram for Sar determination by microfluidic platform and corresponding fitting curve.
Illustration of smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform for Sar detection.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor and smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform based on oxidation induced Ce(III)/Ce(IV) phosphatase-like activity and complexation effect activation for sarcosine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors who equally contribute to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Prostate cancer (PCa) has seriously affected men's quality of life and even endangered their lives. Thus, precise and reliable determination of related biomarkers in clinical samples is extremely important for early diagnosis and treatment of PCa. Sarcosine (Sar) is widely regarded as a potential biomarker for non-invasive diagnosis of PCa owing to its specific high-level expression in the development and metastasis of PCa. With the aid of Ce(III) as a bridge between 4-Methylumbelliferyl Phosphate (4-MUP) and orange emitted carbon dots (o-CDs), here we have constructed a new ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Sar determination by reasonably combining the phosphatase-like activity and complexation reaction characteristics of Ce(IV). This sensor indicates high sensitivity, exceptional selectivity, good reliability, and wide linear range of 0.2–120 μM with a detection limit of 89 nM. Besides, our proposed sensor demonstrates excellent precise and stability even in complex actual urine samples. Interestingly, an inexpensive, portable but reliable smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform has been fabricated and successfully employed for precise determination of Sar in urine samples, which offers a powerful and convenient tool for the early diagnosis of PCa, and even inspires the research and development of other practical optical sensing devices for cancer home screening.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer (PCa) is one of the most widespread malignant tumors in male worldwide, and the 1414,259 new cases and 375,304 deaths were counted in 2020 [1,2]. More seriously, the death toll from prostate cancer will reach almost 740,000 by 2040 according to the Global Cancer Observatory (GCO) [3]. Hence, early and precise diagnosis of PCa possesses important implications for the improvement of survival rate and reduction of treatment costs. Unfortunately, practical common screening methods for PCa such as blood prostate specific antigen (PSA) detection and digital rectal examination (DRE) have a high false positive rate and extreme discomfort caused by invasive testing, which causes huge physiological and psychological pressure and treatment costs for patients [4,5]. Therefore, it is extremely urgent to choose more suitable biomarkers and develop more accurate non-invasive diagnostic approaches for PCa screening.

In recent years, sarcosine (Sar) has aroused tremendous interest among researchers owing to its specific high-level expression in the development and metastasis of PCa. More importantly, extensive and reliable research confirms that the differential expression of Sar can even accurately distinguish different stages of PCa [6,7]. Therefore, Sar can be regarded as a potential biomarker for non-invasive diagnosis of PCa. Currently, various analytical approaches, based on chromatographic [8,9], electrochemical [7,10], colorimetric [11,12], and fluorometric methods [13,14], have been explored and applied to the determination of Sar. Especially, ratiometric fluorescence strategies, which plays a core role in the research and development of portable visual detection devices due to their excellent self-calibration function and abundant color variations, have received widely concerns [15,16]. Microfluidic chip is an intelligent laboratory system that integrates injection, reaction, separation, and analysis, enabling meticulously designed ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy to determine target with high-throughput and automation [17,18]. With the powerful color capture and processing functions of smartphones, fluorescence color information can be output more intuitively and converted into target content [19,20]. Obviously, the smartphone-based microfluidic sensing system can achieve real-time and rapid detection of disease markers, which possesses huge potential for portable and accurate early diagnosis of diseases.

The phosphatase mimicking performance of various cerium nanomaterials such as CeO2 nanorods, single-atom cerium, and Ce-MOF have been reported one after another and have attracted considerable attention [21–23]. Both Ce3+ and Ce4+ coexist extensively on the surface of these nanoceria, nevertheless extensive studies have indicated strong phosphatase like activity of Ce4+, while Ce3+ have almost no catalytic activity [24]. More interestingly, Ce4+ can exhibit powerful complexation to quench the fluorescence of certain fluorescent substances compared with Ce3+ [25,26]. Cleverly combining the above two characteristics of Ce3+/Ce4+ redox couples and utilizing them reasonably to construct optical sensors with dual signal outputs have broad promising for detection of disease markers, especially Sar. In this research, the H2O2 generated from the catalytic oxidation of Sar by SOX can oxidize Ce(III) to generate Ce(IV) with phosphatase-like activity, leading to the hydrolysis of non-fluorescent 4-MUP to yield blue luminescent 4-MU, while orange fluorescence of o-CDs is simultaneously quenched owing to complexation effect of Ce(IV). In view of this, the Ce(III) can be exquisitely served as a bridge between 4-MUP and o-CDs, and phosphatase-like activity and complexation reaction characteristic of Ce(IV) are reasonably combined to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Sar determination. Notably, we have fabricated a smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform that integrates microfluidic chip, 3D-printing device, and smartphone together, and achieves highly sensitive, reliable, but convenient analysis of Sar in urine samples ( Scheme 1), which provides a stable and convenient tool for the early diagnosis of PCa, and even offers inspiration for the research and development of other portable disease diagnosis devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of orange emitted carbon dots (o-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The o-CDs were prepared with slight modification according to reported literature [27]. In detail, 500 mg Rhodamine B was fully dissolved into 20 mL ultrapure water by ultrasound. The above mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 160 ℃ for 2 h. After the reaction, the obtained crimson liquid was successively centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with 0.22 µm membranes to eliminate large particle impurities. Subsequently, the o-CDs solution was dialyzed by using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off 1000) for removal of excessive small molecules. Ultimately, the solid o-CDs were acquired by freeze-drying means for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of H2O2 and Sar
ce:section.ce:para: For H2O2 assay, 50 μL 4-MUP (80 μM), 100 μL Ce3+ (20 mM), 100 μL o-CDs (0.2 mg mL−1), 100 μL H2O2 at various concentrations, and 650 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM) were mixed and incubated at 37 ℃ for 8 min. After reaction, the mixture was transferred into the quartz cuvette for fluorescent test. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were acquired under 390 nm excitation light.

For Sar assay, 100 μL SOX (10 U·mL−1), 100 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM), and 100 μL Sar at various levels were incubated together at 37 ℃ for 30 min. And then 50 μL 4-MUP (80 μM), 100 μL Ce3+ (20 mM), 100 μL o-CDs (0.2 mg mL−1), and 450 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM) were added and continued to incubate for 8 min, following by fluorescence test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Sar via smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The construction and application of smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform have driven the pace of portability and high-throughput detection of Sar. In detail, 30 μL SOX (10 U·mL−1), 30 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM), and 30 μL Sar (various levels) were successively added to visual detection chamber via microfluidic channels, followed by incubating at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Subsequently, 15 μL 4-MUP (80 μM), 30 μL Ce3+ (20 mM), 30 μL o-CDs (0.2 mg mL−1), and 135 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM) were introduced and incubated for another 8 min. Afterwards, the microfluidic chip was transferred to smartphone assisted 3D printing device for detection. The fluorescent color information was captured and converted into corresponding RGB values by application program “Color Grab” installed on smartphone. Eventually, the Sar content was calculated and calibrated via obtained fitting formula.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Sar in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The supernatant of urine samples from normal volunteers was collected by centrifugation and subsequently diluted 20-fold with Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 50 mM), followed by spiking with given different contents of Sar. Ultimately, the above spiked samples were tested via the identical operation procedure as 2.2 and 2.3 sections.

Utilizing Rhodamine B as raw material, the o-CDs were obtained through a facile hydrothermal approach, and the synthetic process was illustrated in Fig. 1A. Then the as-synthetized o-CDs were systematically certified by various characterization techniques. Similar to other reported CQDs [28], the diﬀraction peaks of o-CDs dovetail nicely with those of their precursor. Among them, an acromion centered nearly 22° exhibits in the XRD spectrum o-CDs, indicating their amorphous structure (Fig.S1) [29,30]. Moreover, the sharp peak appearing at 17° may belong to the characteristic diffraction peak of Rhodamine B. Meanwhile, the o-CDs exhibit subglobose particle morphology with mean particle size of approximately 2.6 nm and are uniformly distributed on TEM image (Fig. 1B and Fig.S2). The hydrodynamic particle size of o-CDs was also measured for cross validation (Fig.S3), and the measured value is 4.33 nm, which is slightly larger than statistical result from TEM image because of Brownian movement and hydration effect in the liquid [31]. Besides, the Zeta potential measurement for o-CDs is − 2.40 mV, indicating its negative charge (Fig.S4). To study rich chemical functional groups on o-CDs, the relevant FT-IR tests were carried out. As depicted in Fig. 1C, a broad and strong peak at around 3428 cm−1 originates from O-H stretching vibration, and absorption band at 2910 cm−1 revels the existence of C-H stretching vibration. The vibration bands at around 2376 and 1076 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N group. Meanwhile, the distinct absorption band centered at 1595 cm−1 is associated with CO/CC functional group. The absorption peaks appearing at 1420 and 1335 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C-C band. Furthermore, the minor absorption peaks at approximately 1251 and 1184 cm−1 should be assigned to the stretching vibration of C-O band [32]. These above FT-IR results illustrate the surface of o-CDs is rich in carboxy and amino group. Also, XPS technology was employed to further investigate the surface chemical information of o-CDs. Evidently, the survey XPS spectrum manifests that o-CDs are composed of C, N, O elements (Fig.S5). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum displays the bonding information of C-C/CC (283.3 eV), C-N (284.3 eV), C-O-C (284.9 eV), CO (286.0 eV), and COOH (287.0 eV) in o-CDs (Fig. 1D). Besides, as seen from the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum, four peaks situated in 398.1, 399.6, 400.3, and 401.1 eV are attributed to N = C, C-N-C, N-H and N-(C)3 groups, respectively (Fig. 1E). More importantly, the O 1 s spectrum appears two peaks at 531.1 and 532.2 eV, representing C-O and CO groups respectively (Fig. 1F) [27,32]. The above XPS analysis is in accordance with FT-IR results. Thus, all the outcomes verify the successful synthesis of o-CDs, and o-CDs have abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups.

Next, the optical properties of as-synthetized o-CDs were studied in detail. As observed from UV−vis spectrum of o-CDs, a weak peak appearing at ∼350 nm is ascribed to the n − π * electron transition of CO group. Besides, a distinct absorption peak at ∼550 nm should be assigned to n–π * transition related with lone pair electron on the edge N atoms of heptazine-based structure [33]. Visibly, the peach red o-CDs solution appears an extremely bright orange fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (the inset in Fig. 1G). Moreover, the optimal excitation and emission peaks of o-CDs are positioned at approximately 555 and 580 nm, respectively (Fig. 1G). And the position of emission peak hardly shifts as the excitation signal changes in the range of 520–570 nm, demonstrating the excitation-independent emission behavior of o-CDs (Fig. 1H) [34]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of o-CDs is calculated to be 53.8% by utilizing Rhodamine B as the standard [35,36]. Such high quantum yield is attributed to the presence of electron-rich oxygenous and nitrogenous functional groups on o-CDs, which are commonly regard as outstanding auxiliary chromophores [37,38].

Optical stability is an important index for evaluating the performance of fluorescent probe [39,40]. Several possible influence factors such as solution pH, ionic strength, irradiation time, and storage time were selected to evaluate the optical stability of o-CDs. As depicted in Fig.S6, the FL intensity of o-CDs over a wide range of pH 4–10 remains almost constant except for slight fluorescence quenching at pH= 3. The ideal optical stability of o-CDs in such a wide pH range endows them valuable value and wide applications, especially for disease analysis at physiological pH. The ionic strength influence on FL intensity of o-CDs is presented in Fig.S7. The FL intensity of o-CDs remains nearly constant with the increase of NaCl level from 0 to 0.3 M, which presents that o-CDs are stable even in high-salt environments. Fig.S8 compares the FL intensities of o-CDs exposed to 365 nm UV light for 120 min. No obvious change on the FL intensity of o-CDs is observed throughout the irradiation time, demonstrating strong photo-bleaching resistance of o-CDs. Meanwhile, the FL intensity of o-CDs reveals no distinct change throughout 30 days storage in dark at 4 °C (Fig.S9), suggesting excellent storage stability of o-CDs. These foregoing outcomes indicate that o-CD can be fully served as an ideal optical probe for construction of optical sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of o-CDs after interacting with Ce(IV)
ce:section.ce:para: 4-methylumbelliferyl phosphate (4-MUP) is a typical fluorescent substrate of alkaline phosphatase (ALP), whose phosphate ester substituents are hydrolyzed by phosphatase to produce 4-methylumbelliferone (4-MU) with intensive blue fluorescence [41,42]. Surprisingly, after 4-MUP is added into H2O2 +Ce(III) system and incubated together, a distinct fluorescence peak appears at 450 nm and the solution manifests strong blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. However, no visible fluorescence signal at 450 nm is measured in 4-MUP, 4-MUP+H2O2, and 4-MUP+Ce(III) system (Fig.S10B). The occurrence of this phenomenon can be speculated that Ce(III) ions are oxidized by H2O2 to produce Ce(IV) ions with phosphatase-like activity, leading to the hydrolysis of 4-MUP to yield 4-MU (Fig.S10A). To test this hypothesis, the fluorescence spectra of Ce(IV) and 4-MUP+Ce(IV) system were recorded and compared. As observed from Fig.S11, a remarkable fluorescence signal occurs in the 4-MUP+Ce (IV) system, while Ce(IV) alone exhibits no fluorescence signal, which is consistent with our inference. Whereas, the 4-MUP+Ce(III) system was naturally applied to the quantitative determination of H2O2 and relevant test results were presented in Fig.S12. The FL response of 4-MUP+Ce(III) system (F448) has a reluctantly acceptable linear fitting relationship with H2O2 content in the scope of 0.08–40 μM, accompanied by subtle changes in the brightness of blue fluorescence. Thereby, the 4-MUP+Ce(III) system exists several deficiencies during H2O2 detection process, such as (1) The single-signal channel sensor is susceptible to diverse environmental factors and may secretly output erroneous analysis results in complex matrix samples; (2) The fluorescence sensor with single-signal output fails to offer fluorescence color variations visible to the naked eye, which inevitably hinders its further application in micro visualization sensing device. Therefore, the design of fluorescence sensors with multiple-signal outputs manifests extraordinary significance. Interestingly, the H2O2 +Ce(III) system can quench the fluorescence of o-CDs, while H2O2 or Ce(III) alone has no effect (Fig.S13). The fluorescence quenching of o-CDs may be caused by Ce(IV) generated through the oxidation reaction between Ce(III) and H2O2, which is identical to the outcomes of Fig.S14.

Several techniques were adopted to further explore the impact mechanism of Ce(IV) on o-CDs. As demonstrated by Fig. 2 A, the o-CDs reveal a visible trend of aggregation after interacting with Ce(IV). Correspondingly, the increase of the hydrodynamic particle size also confirms this point (Fig.S15). The UV–vis spectra of o-CDs and o-CDs+Ce(IV) with different contents (Fig. 2B) display that the absorption peak of o-CDs at ∼550 nm weakens or even disappears with the increase of Ce(IV) addition, accompanied by the fading of peach red o-CDs solution (Fig.S16), which testifies the formation of new chemical bond [43]. Instead, the o-CDs + Ce(III) system manifests no visible color conversion and characteristic peak at ∼550 nm is not affected compared with o-CDs alone system (Fig.S17), which further excludes the potential impact of Ce(III) on o-CDs. Furthermore, the appearance of new Ce-O bonds on the FT-IR spectrum strongly demonstrates the formation of non-fluorescent complex between Ce(IV) and o-CDs (Fig. 2 C) [44], which has been also cross validated by XPS testing. As proved by Fig.S18, the Ce, O, N, and C elements exist in this complex, among which the peak signal of Ce element is extremely weak due to its content accounting for only 0.08%. The coexistence of Ce3+ and Ce4+ (Fig. 2D) indicates electron transfer from O and N to Ce4+, and further testifying the formation of Ce(IV)-o-CDs complex. Apparently, the C 1 s peak has slightly shifted from 284 eV to 284.5 eV after the introduction of Ce(IV) (Fig. 2E). Likewise, the O 1 s peak also appears a slight shift and a new peak attributed to Ce-O emerges at 530.8 eV (Fig. 2F). The overall N 1 s peak signal of o-CDs after treatment with Ce(IV) is also slightly shifted to the direction of high electron binding energy (Fig. 2G). The varieties of O 1 s and N 1 s spectra further prove the electron transfer from O and N to Ce4+, which leads to the decrease of their electron cloud densities and thus higher binding energies [45]. Thereby, the foregoing outcomes fully testify that Ce(IV) can quench the fluorescence of o-CDs by forming non-fluorescent complex with o-CDs. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of o-CDs is around 1.61 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime converts to 1.54 ns after the introduction of Ce(IV), indicating the fluorescence quenching of o-CDs towards Ce(IV) is a dynamic procedure (Fig.S19) [26].

After the quenching mechanism was confirmed, the o-CDs+Ce(III) system was attempted for H2O2 determination (Fig. 2H). The reluctantly acceptable fitting linear curve and extremely inconspicuous fluorescence color variance (Fig.S20) tremendously limit the development of FL sensor based on single o-CDs towards stable and reliable portable fluorescent sensing devices. Fortunately, the Ce(III) can be exquisitely served as a bridge between 4-MUP and o-CDs fluorescence signal sources, and phosphatase-like activity and complexation reaction characteristic of Ce(IV) are reasonably combined to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for H2O2 determination. To verify the feasibility of 4-MUP+o-CDs hybrid sensing system, the related fluorescence experiments were conducted. As depicted in Fig. 3A, a strong fluorescence signal appears at 448 nm and the fluorescence peak at 578 nm is evidently weakened after Ce(III) and H2O2 are introduced to 4-MUP+o-CDs system, while the Ce(III) or H2O2 alone present ignorable effect on the 4-MUP+o-CDs system (Fig.S21), which is attributed to the role of Ce(IV) generated through the redox reaction between Ce(III) and H2O2, and confirmed in Fig.S22. Specifically, the H2O2 triggers the oxidation of Ce(III) to Ce(IV) which not only possesses phosphatase-like activity to hydrolyze 4-MUP to produce 4-MU with blue fluorescence, but also reveals strong chelation effect on o-CDs, leading to their fluorescence quenching (Fig. 3B). Therefore, this cleverly designed ratiometric fluorometric sensor possesses accurate self-calibration function, distinct fluorescent color variations, and exceptional analytical performance, which can be used for sensing of H2O2 and related upstream molecules, and even emerges immense potential in the exploration of portable fluorescence devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism and detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire better analysis capabilities, the dosage of Ce(III) and incubation time were optimized. As displayed in Fig.S23, the FL intensity of 4-MUP+H2O2 system initially remarkably enhances with the increase of Ce(III) level, and reaches dynamic equilibrium at Ce(III) concentration of 2 mM. Conversely, the FL signal of o-CDs+H2O2 system decreases with Ce(III) raising, and just arrives at dynamic balance when Ce(III) content is up to 20 mM. To simultaneously consider the optimal state of two signal probes, Ce(III) concentration was selected as 20 mM for subsequent experiments. Similarly, the 8 min was chosen as optimal incubation time (Fig.S24). Subsequently, the ratiometric fluorometric sensor based on 4-MUP+Ce(III)+o-CDs system was applied to the sensing of H2O2 under optimal reaction conditions. As expected, the fluorescence signal at 448 nm raises with the increasing H2O2 content, while the fluorescence peak of o-CDs at 578 nm slowly decreases (Fig. 3 C). Accordingly, the fluorescence ratio (F578/F448) is negatively correlated with H2O2 level in the range of 0.08–50 μM (Fig. 3D), accompanied by rich color variances from orange to blue-purple (Fig. 3E). Interestingly, a terrific linear response is attained between 1/(F578/F448) and H2O2 level in the scope of 0.08–50 μM, and the linear fitting equation is expressed as 1/(F578/F448) = 0.1477 + 0.0353[H2O2] (R2 = 0.993) with the limit of detection (LOD) of 38 nM (Fig. 3 F). Currently, extensive research has been focused on colorimetric/fluorometric H2O2 sensors based on peroxidase mimic and the recent strategies are presented in Table S1. Compared to these approaches, our proposed method can complete the test in just several minutes with comparable or even better detection performance, which drastically reduces the analysis time and experimental process.

Relying on SOX-induced catalytic oxidation of Sar to produce H2O2, the established ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy above is attempted for Sar assay ( Fig. 4A). As testified by Fig. 4B, Sar+SOX also affects the fluorescent signal of 4-MUP+o-CDs+Ce(III) system, which should be attributed to generated H2O2 via enzyme-catalyzed reaction between Sar and SOX. Nevertheless, the SOX or Sar alone has negligible effects on the fluorescence signal of 4-MUP+Ce(III) and o-CDs+Ce(III) system (Fig.S25 and Fig.S26), and even on the composite system of 4-MUP+o-CDs+Ce(III) (Fig.S27), which fully demonstrates the feasibility of fabricated sensing platform for Sar determination. Unsurprisingly, the fluorescence signal at 448 nm appears and gradually enhances as the Sar level raises, while the fluorescent intensity of o-CDs at 578 nm slowly weakens (Fig. 4C). Correspondingly, a negative correlation relationship between F578/F448 and Sar concentration ranging from 0.2 to 150 μM is presented (Fig. 4D). Encouragingly, the reciprocal of FL intensity ratio (1/(F578/F448)) is linearly with Sar content in the scope of 0.2–120 μM, along with brilliant color difference variation from orange to blue-purple (Fig. 4E), and the regression equation is expressed as 1/(F578/F448) = 0.2059 + 0.0111[Sar] (R2 = 0.992) with the LOD of 89 nM (Fig. 4F). By compared with those reported previously methods in Table S2, our proposed Sar sensing strategy manifests acceptable and even superior assay performance.

To assess the selectivity of developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Sar assay, several possible coexisting interferent were selected for research. Compared to the target, 5-fold of these interferents present almost negligible impact on this detection platform (Fig. 4G), suggesting that our established sensor can be applied to Sar determination with outstanding selectivity. To estimate the practicability and solidity of designed biosensor, the analyte Sar in healthy male urine samples were tested via spiked means. Specifically, the spiked samples were first prepared by mixing diluted 20 times urine with diverse level of Sar, and then determined via our constructed biosensor. The correlative test outcomes are organized and presented in Table S3, the fully acceptable recovery rates (92.0%∼112.0%) and the determination results consistent with commercial kit proclaim that our strategy possesses fine feasibility and stability for actual samples detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform for Sar determination
ce:section.ce:para: The smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform composed of a microfluidic chip, a 3D-printing device, a smartphone, and a 365 nm UV lamp is designed and constructed under AutoCAD 2019 software, 3D printing technique, and microfluidic processing technique ( Fig. 5A). Among them, this microfluidic chip made of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) reveals a small disk with 30 mm (diameter) × 10 mm (height), which divides into a visual detection chamber (10 mm diameter) and six injection channels (2 mm (diameter) × 10 mm (length)). Furthermore, the 3D-printing device prepared from black resin displays an approximate outline of 75 mm (length) × 60 mm (width) × 112 mm (height), which fixes the smartphone and 365 nm UV lamp to ensure the smoothness of detection process (Fig.S28). The smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform for Sar determination is realized via the following three procedures: signal acquisition, RGB analysis, and outcome output. The first step is to perform a series of reactions in the microfluidic chip, and detailed process is presented in 2.3 section; The second step is to quickly capture the output fluorescent color signal through “Color Grab” APP on the smartphone and convert it into RGB values; The third step is to establish the correlation between RGB value and Sar content, and obtain a suitable linear fitting equation. Visibly, the blue channel (B) value appears a good linear correlation with the logarithm of Sar content in the scope of 0.2–150 μM, and the regression equation is fitted as [B] = 88.936 + 22.842[Sar] (R2 = 0.995) with the LOD of 127 nM (Fig. 5B). Besides, the reliability and practicability of microfluidic sensing platform was estimated through spiking experiments in actual samples, and the relevant experimental steps were presented in 2.4 section. The obtained outcomes are listed in the Table S4, and the acceptable recovery values (95.7%∼115.0%) and the determination results consistent with commercial kit proclaim demonstrate the reliability and stability of this microfluidic sensing platform in practical applications. More importantly, this simple but practical smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform can serve as a powerful tool for real-time detection of Sar and even other biomarkers, which has profound implications for personal health and even public safety.

To sum up, with the aid of Ce(III) as a bridge between 4-MUP and o-CD, a new ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Sar determination has been constructed by reasonably combining the phosphatase-like activity and complexation reaction characteristics of Ce(IV). This sensing strategy reveals expected analytical performance for Sar with good sensitivity and selectivity as well as excellent reliability, and still demonstrates excellent precise and stability even in complex actual urine samples. More importantly, a low-cost, portable but reliable smartphone-based microfluidic sensing platform has been constructed and successfully utilized for the precise determination of Sar in urine samples. As a result, our efforts not only offer a novel and efficient tool for accurate and convenient early diagnosis of PCa, but also inspire the research and development of other practical optical sensing platforms for cancer home screening.

Linjie Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shujun Zheng: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Zhihao Cai: Formal analysis, Validation. Fei Wang: Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Caolong Li: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region (XZ202301YD0018C). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, fluorescence lifetime tests, and so on.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134461.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

N. J. Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Linjie Wang is a doctoral candidate at China Pharmaceutical University, and he focuses on the fabrication of sensing systems using nanozymes.
Shujun Zheng is a master candidate under the supervision of Dr. Li and Dr. Wang, and she focuses on biochemical analysis based on nanozymes.
Zhihao Cai is a master candidate under the supervision of Dr. Li and Dr. Wang, and he is interested in detection of disease markers based on nanozymes.

Fei Wang is an associated professor at China Pharmaceutical University. He shows his favor in the fabrication of functional nanomaterials, and is deeply absorbed in the corresponding applications of medical analysis and fluorescence devices.
Caolong Li is a professor at China Pharmaceutical University, and he focuses on the functionalization of nanomaterials in medical analysis, biomedical imaging, gas sensing, and fluorescence devices.
